observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to the following courses of action:

a. For the General Services Office (GSO) to:
i. prepare and maintain records for the receipts and issuances of the
supplies and/or inventory items in the corresponding Stock Cards;
ii. prepare the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI)
and forward to the Municipal Accountant’s Office;
iii. conduct physical count of inventory items semi-annually and
subsequently prepare the RPCI for submission to the audit team
not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and
second semesters, respectively;
iv. design and implement a systematic process for tracking fuel
consumption and ensuring timely recording of usage to maintain
accuracy of the inventory account;

b. For the Municipal Accountant’s Office to:
i. prepare and maintain Supply Ledger Cards (SLC) for each
inventory account;
ii. require that all purchases of inventory items shall course through
the inventory accounts and issuances thereof shall be recorded as
they take place, in reference to the necessary supporting
documents;
iii. reconcile the RPCI with the accounting records to establish the
existence, completeness and accuracy of the inventory balances
reported in the financial reports; and
iv. effect the necessary adjusting entry to derecognize the improperly
recorded Fuel, Oil, and Lubricants Inventory account from the
books of accounts. The corresponding pro-forma entry is shown
below:

Account Title Debit Credit
Accounts Payable or Prior Period Adjustment* xxx
Fuel, Oil and Lubricants Inventory xxx
To derecognize the improperly recorded Fuel, Oil, and Lubricants
Inventory account
* Accounts Payable shall be used for the undelivered portion of POs, while Prior
Period Adjustment shall be used for the portion already delivered or withdrawn
by the LGU from the supplier’s fuel depot or gasoline station.

We recommended the following courses of action:
1. For the Municipal Mayor to:
a) strictly follow and observe the procedural guidelines to facilitate the one-time
cleansing of PPE account balances in accordance with COA Circular no. 2020-
006 dated Jan 31, 2020, in conducting physical inventory;

2. For the Inventory Committee to:
b) prepare and submit an approved Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) by the head of
the agency to the COA to ensure an efficient and organized Physical Inventory
Count. The PIP should be prepared based on the inventory working papers,
namely: (a) the latest RPCPPE or Inventory Count Form; (b) updated PPELCs
duly reconciled with the General Ledgers; (c) list of PPE items recorded in the
PPELCs but not included in the RPCPPE; and (d) list of PPE items included in
the RPCPPE but not recorded in the PPELCs;
c) execute the inventory taking activities in accordance with the PIP;
d) tag all PPE items counted with new property stickers, as provided under
Paragraph 5.7 of COA Circular No. 2020-006, to facilitate easier and more
accurate physical inventory counts and reconciliation with accounting records;
e) prepare the RPCPPE after completion of the physical count;
f) expedite the disposal of unserviceable properties in accordance with the
guidelines/policies stipulated in Sec. 79 of PD 1445 and COA-DBM Joint
Circular No. 2024-1 to deter further deterioration of such assets;
g) initiate the transfer of ownership of PPE items which are already in
possession of and controlled by end-users other than the Municipality and
comply with the necessary documentary requirements; or, alternatively, the
LGU may execute a Deed of Usufruct to formally authorize the concerned end-
users to utilize the properties while retaining ownership of the said properties.

3. For the Municipal Accountant to:
h) effect the necessary journal entries to recognize all PPE items physically
found at the station and to derecognize non-existing/missing PPE items from
the books, provided that all the supporting documents required under COA
Circular No. 2020-006 are duly complied with;
i) ensure derecognition of PPE items from the books of account of the
Municipality once the transfer of ownership of other PPE items has been
completed, in compliance with IPSAS 17;
j) ensure that the proper accounting and reporting procedures for all
acquisition/issuance/transfer/disposal of government assets are pursuant to the
existing manuals and other relevant laws, rules and regulations after the
completion of the one-time cleansing; and

4. For the Municipal Accountant and General Service Officer to:
k) undertake collaborative procedures to ensure that all PPEs included in the
RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records and that the Property
Cards (PCs) maintained by the Property Unit and the PPELCs maintained by
the Accounting Unit are reconciled.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Office of the Municipal
Accountant:
a. in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer’s Office, conduct a thorough
review, confirmation and document gathering of all outstanding accounts
payable, particularly those aged over two (2) years to determine which of the
payables are still valid and bona fide obligations of the Municipality;
b. prepare the necessary adjusting entries to revert accounts payable with no
valid claims and have remained dormant for over two (2) years to the
unappropriated surplus of the general fund to have a more reliable and
accurate financial reporting; and
c. review the validity of unliquidated obligations or accounts payable on a
regular basis and establish control mechanisms to monitor the timely
settlement and avoid the build-up of dormant payables.

We recommended and Management agreed that:

a) the MDRRM Council, prepare the LDRRMF Investment Plan for all
available balances of the DRRM Fund for the year, including transfers to
the Special Trust Fund. Regularly review and update such plans to
consider current vulnerabilities and timely implement those approved pre-
disaster PPAs;
b) the implementing departments, through the DRRM Officer, follow the
schedule of implementation of pre-disaster PPAs as embodied in the
approved DRRM Investment Plan to avoid delays in the implementation
of disaster risk management projects;
c) the DRRM Officer, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, to
monitor all sources of fund, including the STF, to maximize funding of
DRRM-related PPAs;
d) the MDRRM Council, through the DRRM Officer, evaluate the status of
those unimplemented PPAs if the same are still to be pursued, otherwise
consider their realignment or reversion to other necessary DRRM PPAs;
e) the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the MEO and MDRRMO,
reevaluate the documents and reports related to the pending projects
under the Special Trust Fund to properly report the actual status of
implementation. After which, revert the unutilized balances of STF from
CY 2017 and prior to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund;
f) the Municipal Accountant prepare the Monthly Report on Sources and
Utilization of DRRMF, ensure that the details reconcile with other
financial reports and disclose in the notes to financial statements any other
information necessary to substantiate the variances between the books of
account and other financial records; and
g) the Municipal Accountant to submit the Monthly Report on Sources and
Utilization of DRRMF to the audit team within the prescribed period.

We recommended and Management agreed that:

a) the Local Chief Executive direct the implementing departments to
prioritize the implementation of the PPAs based on the scheduled time
frames indicated in the AIP to improve the utilization and completion
rate of the 20% Local Development Fund and to avoid the accumulation
of more unimplemented projects; and

b) the Municipal Development Council, in coordination with the Municipal
Planning and Development Office, further evaluate the remaining
unimplemented projects under the 20% Local Development Fund which
could be realigned or augmented to other priority projects that are more
feasible, provided that the existing rules and regulations are being
followed.

observations.recommendation:
In view of its partially/not implemented status as reflected in the prior year's
Annual Audit Report, the following recommendations are reiterated and the
management agreed for the Local Chief Executive to:

a) require and provide the necessary support for the identification of PDAF
balances that have remained outstanding in the books for several years. If
properly identified, ensure immediate settlement of such total balance by
remitting it to the national treasury so that funds can be utilized by the
national government;

b) require the implementing offices or departments to draw up the appropriate
project proposals to utilize fully idle or dormant trust funds pursuant to the
law governing said grant and/or the Memorandum of Agreement/contract
with the respective source agencies;

c) direct the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Heads of
implementing departments, to implement proper liquidation of the amount
granted as well as the refund of excess project funds to the source agency or
request for transfer to its General Fund, if so provided in the terms of the
grant;

d) require the implementing offices to execute without delay the intended PPAs
of the fund transfers so that the benefits derived therefrom may already be
enjoyed by the intended beneficiaries;

e) direct the Municipal Accountant to properly classify and record the
transactions in its appropriate account titles (under corresponding subsidiary
ledgers) to have a more reliable balance of accounts to be presented in the
financial statements;

f) henceforth, every implementing department should ensure the existence of
necessary documents such as agreements, other related guidelines or
approved work and financial plan of every fund received for proper
recording, timely implementation and effective monitoring;

g) direct the Municipal Accountant to refund excess project funds to the source
agencies or request for the transfer to the General Fund according to the law
governing said grant and/or the Memorandum of Agreement/contract with
the respective source agencies;

h) require the Accounting Department, in coordination with the implementing
departments, to monitor and analyze the records in the ledger and properly
classify and specifically identify those inter-agency transferred funds and
collections. Henceforth, the treasury department to provide specific
identification of fund transfers and collections upon issuance of receipt and
the accounting department to observe proper recording and identification of
funds in the ledger by requiring the necessary documents such as agreements
or other related guidelines from the responsible and concerned department/s
or offices; and

i) require the implementing departments to provide an approved work and
financial plan for each collection generated and held in trust for specific
purposes. Execute without delay the intended programs, projects and
activities of the fund transfers and other specific-purpose collections so that
the benefits expected therefrom may already be enjoyed by the intended
beneficiaries.

In addition thereto, the following new recommendations are given:

j) require the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the accounting
department, to observe timely and full remittance of all collections accruing
to the national government in accordance with the existing laws, rules and
regulations; and

k) the Accounting Department, in coordination with the implementing
departments to perform reconciliation and record the necessary adjusting
entries of those dormant/non-moving fund transfers. Once reconciled but
there are still remaining unadjusted balances, gather the required documents
necessary to file the request for authority to write-off dormant fund transfers
such as certification by the chief accountants and approved by the heads of
the source and implementing agencies that the fund was utilized for the
purpose, and certification from the recipient that the project was partially or
fully implemented, supported by pictures of the implemented projects.

In view of its partially implemented status, as reflected in the prior year’s
Annual Audit Report, we reiterated to recommend and the management agreed that:

a) the MDRRM Council, headed by the Municipal Mayor, prepare the LDRRMF
Investment Plan for all available balances of the DRRM Fund for the year,
including transfers to the Special Trust Fund. Regularly review and update
such plans to consider current vulnerabilities and implement timely those
approved pre-disaster PPAs;

b) the implementing departments headed by the Municipal Mayor through the
DRRM Officer, follow the schedule of implementation of pre-disaster PPAs as
embodied in the approved DRRM Investment Plans. In coordination with the
Engineering Department, evaluate the causes of delays in the implementation
of pre-disaster projects in order to provide immediate resolution therein and
lessen or eliminate the vulnerabilities of the community in a timely manner;

c) the MDRRM Council through the DRRM Officer, in coordination with the
Municipal Accounting Department, properly identify the remaining balances
of the DRRM Fund as a result of fully completed PPAs and considers its re-
appropriation to other priority DRRM PPAs that remained unfunded. Also,
evaluate the status of those unimplemented PPAs if the same is still to be
pursued, otherwise consider its realignment or reversion to other necessary
DRRM PPAs; and

d) the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Engineering
Office, to reevaluate the documents and reports related to the pending projects
under the Special Trust Fund to properly identify the expenses that should be
charged against the fund. After which, revert the unutilized balances of STF
from CY 2017 and below to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund.

To address the new audit observations, we also recommended and the
management agreed that:

e) the Municipal Accountant prepare the monthly Report on Sources and
Utilization of DRRMF and ensures that the details reconcile with other
financial reports; and

f) the MDRRM Officer submit to COA the Report on Sources and Utilization of
DRRMF within the prescribed period.

We recommended and the management agreed that the Local Chief Executive:

a) direct the implementing departments to prioritize the implementation of
development projects and ensure that allocated funds are timely and optimally
utilized for projects identified in accordance with the guidelines prescribed
under the DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-01; and

b) direct the Local Development Council to review the
unobligated/unprogrammed appropriations of the LDF, determine the status
of implementation of the projects by coordinating with the implementing
departments and consider the recommendation of reversion of the savings
from continuing appropriations to be made available for other priority
development projects which shall be derived from the local development plan.

Therefore, we recommended that the accountable officers will do the
following to ensure proper and timely recording of the transactions as well as avoid
unliquidated cash advances in the hands of the accountable officer and the possibility
of misuse of funds:
1. For the Municipal Accountant to:
a. stop granting a single cash advance with different purposes to
disbursing officer;
b. refrain from granting additional cash advances to disbursing officer
without settlement of the previous cash advance
2. For the Municipal Treasurer to:
a. Develop a control whereby giving ample/reasonable number of days
for payees to receive their claims. In the event that the payee is not
able to claim on a specified date, the amount should be refunded by
the disbursing officer, evidenced by an official receipt, to ensure
timely liquidation of the cash advances and recording by the Municipal
Accountant Upon request of the claimant, the Municipal Treasurer will then issue
a check payable to the claimant.
b. Seek approval from the Local Chief Executive for an additional
Disbursing Officer or Special Disbursing Officer to cater other cash
advances to ensure that payments will not be delayed due to late
liquidation of previous cash advances.
3. For the Disbursing Officer to:
a. stop partial submission of paid claims / vouchers;
b. prepare and submit the Report of Disbursement on fully paid /
settled cash advances;
c. promptly and accurately record the actual liquidation of cash
advances in the cashbook

The Municipal Council should prepare a well-defined AIP in accordance with the harmonization of local planning, investment programming, revenue administration, budgeting, and expenditure management guidelines prescribed in the DBM-NEDA-DILG-DOF Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1, series of 2007 dated March 8, 2007.
observations.reason: CY 2018 Plan was already approved. However, improvements to be addressed in CY 2019 Plans.

observations.recommendation: Require the MDRRMO to closely monitor the undertaking of proposed projects out of LDRRMF to avoid/minimize disaster projected and unexpected risks, and enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities at all levels. Provide specific dates for each PPA and ensure all PPAs are provided with schedules. A comprehensive MDRRMF Plan should be prepared to include both the current appropriations and unexpended balance in the Special Trust Fund. Prepare an adjusting entry to correct the reversion entry made in reverting the CY 2013 unexpended balance as per suggested entries.

The management should require the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the preparation of Bank Reconciliation Statements for all bank accounts and submit the same to the Office of the Auditor. Locate and investigate the discrepancies noted and effect the necessary corrections by making necessary adjustments. Corresponding JEVs should be drawn and copy furnished the Auditor's Office to effect the recording of adjustments. Compliance to COA Circular No. 96-011 is strictly enjoined.
observations.reason: Limited accessibility to bank records and difficulty in retrieving needed documents. Some JEVs drawn; some documents lacking.

observations.recommendation: Require the implementing departments and other personnel concerned to liquidate all cash advances at year-end. Strict compliance to COA Circular No. 90-331 dated May 3, 1990.
observations.reason: Liquidation report and its supporting documents for Confidential Fund were submitted to COA Central Office but credit notice were not yet received.

observations.recommendation: The management should instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Development Council to closely monitor the utilization and implementation of the projects in the Annual Investment Plan. Instruct implementing departments to fast track implementation based on scheduled time frames and ensure strict compliance with Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 of DILG and DBM. Consider implementation by contract rather than by administration.
Provide adequate storage facilities to store and safe keep all procured supplies and materials so as to protect them from possible loss or wastage.
observations.reason: Lack of budgetary requirements for improvement of storage/bodega facilities.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Accountant officials be instructed sternly to submit the required financial reports and accounts. Otherwise, the reglementary sanctions as provided in Section 122 of P.D. 1445 shall be imposed.
observations.reason: Delayed submission; some departments/officials still pending. Submission of required documents delayed.

observations.recommendation: The LGU should adhere strictly with the rules and regulations prescribed in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, pursuant to Section 89 of PD 1445, COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
observations.reason: Some personnel are no longer connected with the LGU; credit notices were sent to COA Central Office for some items.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:

a. Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare an adjusting entries to arrive
at the correct balances of accumulated depreciation and net book values.
Below are the suggested journal entries:
DR Prior Period Adjustments P79,197,491.20
CR Accumulated Depreciation2 P79,197,491.20
b. Update the accounting system and conduct a simulation test to check if the
depreciations provided by the said system is accurate. Also, before the
preparation of the Financial Statements, have a sample of the PPE
accounts and compute the depreciation manually to check if the balances
are similar with the generated data.
2 Refer to Annex II-1 for amount of accumulated depreciation that need to be adjusted for each PPE
account.

We recommended the following:

a. Retrieve the records of the unreconciled amounts of PPE accounts and
provide the same with depreciation. If the same cannot be located,
appraise the physically counted assets and make adjustments to the
recorded properties to start anew the property records;
b. Produce the documents showing the correct acquisition cost and
acquisition dates of the assets and immediately reflect them in the books to
compute for the accumulated depreciation and get the correct net book
value; and
c. Produce the documents evidencing that the biological assets were
distributed to the beneficiaries and then prepare a journal entry to drop
them from the books. In the meantime, prepare the following adjusting
entry to transfer the account from Other PPE to Breeding Stock accounts:
DR Breeding Stocks P378,900.00
CR Other PPE P378,900.00

We recommended the following:

a. Create an Inventory Committee composed of two (2) or more permanent
employees including the supply/property officer or a custodian.
b. Conduct an immediate and thorough physical count of PPE. Prepare and
submit the RCPPE and IIRUP for the CY 2017 by following the formats
as provided in Annexes 49 and 51, Volume II, LGU-NGAS Manual.

We recommended and management agreed on the following:

a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to record the receipt and issuance of
constructions materials directly to the Construction Materials Inventory
account instead of directly debiting the Construction in Progress account
pursuant to existing regulations;
b. Require the General Services Officer to properly account and monitor the
receipt and issuance of construction materials in accordance with
regulations;
c. Require the Municipal Engineer to facilitate the prompt submission of the
Certificates of Completion of the completed projects to the Municipal
Accounting Office;
d. Direct the Municipal Accountant to close the completed projects to the
appropriate asset accounts and compute the related depreciation expenses
accordingly; and
e. Record financial transactions in the CIP account based on progress billing
amounts or percentage of completion as stated in the Statement of Work
Accomplished (SWA).

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

a. Confirm the amount of the payables from the list of the identified creditors
to determine which of the payables are still valid and bonafide obligation;
b. Drop from the books of accounts the undocumented obligations /
payables;
c. Make necessary adjustments in the books of accounts if proven to be
without existing creditors for an accurate presentation of the payable
account in the Financial Statements.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive
direct the Municipal Treasurer to do the following to protect the very liquid asset
of the Municipality:

a) Deposit all her collections intact with the Municipality’s authorized
depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day;
b) Deposit partially collections made during the last working day of the year
to the depository bank.
c) Double check deposits made to minimize adjustments

Therefore, we recommended and management agreed on the following:

a. Stop the practice of letting the Job Orders collect in behalf of the regular
employee;
b. Ensure the liquidation of all collections during the day to the liquidating
officer/Municipal Treasurer pursuant to Section 29 of NGAS Volume 1
that is, to remit to the Liquidating Officer all collections made during the
day at the close of each business day for those situated near the
Municipality’s Office and at least once a week or as soon as the
collections reach P5,000.00 for those Collectors whose official station will
take more than one day to travel.

1. Require the Municipal GSO and Municipal Agriculturist, MSWD Officer
to produce the necessary documents such as requisition issue slip,
acknowledgements receipts or other documents evidencing the issuance
and distribution of the stocks.
2. If the documents were made available, require the Municipal Accountant
to prepare adjusting entries to drop the accounts from the books. Below is
the suggested adjusting entries:

CR Food Supplies for Distribution P 24,300.00
Agricultural and Marine Supplies for
Distribution 464,170.00
Agricultural Produce for Distribution 36,751.00
Animal/ Zoological Supplies Inventory 59,998.50
Breeding Stocks 1,849,100.00

We recommended and management agreed on the following:
1. Require the LDRRMC to prepare the LDRRMFIP strictly in accordance with
COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 by incorporating the
detailed plan for the unexpended balance of the 30% Quick Response Fund
(QRF) and 70% MOOE of the LDRRMF for proper utilization of the
LDRRMF.
2. Stop the practice of continuing the PPAs proposed against the previous’ years
QRF and MOOE as all unexpended balance of these accounts were already
lodged in the STF account which will be subjected to new PPAs at the
beginning of the ensuing year.

We recommended and management agreed on the following:
1. Strictly observe compliance with the said circular by submitting a copy of
the monthly Report on the Utilization of LDRRMF, monitor the same and
report only the utilization of the fund.
2. Include a separate field for the utilization and ending balances of the
unexpended LDRRMF of previous years in the Monthly Report, as
required in the format.
3. Publish the Reports on the Sources and Utilizations of LDRRMF and
other documents into the Municipality’s website and conspicuous places
in accordance with DILG Memorandum Circular (MC) No. 2012-141
dated August 9, 2012.

We recommended and management agreed to stop the granting of the
CNA Incentive if all the pre-requisite documents were not fully met as to details
and other percentage reports required. Henceforth, the Municipality should adhere
to the provision cited under DBM Budget Circular No. 2017-3 dated November
13, 2017 or any future Budget Circulars issued by the DBM relative to the
granting of the CNA Incentive.

We recommended and Management agreed to revisit and adhere to the
provisions of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-141 relative to full
disclosure by immediately posting the required reports of local budget and
finances, bids and public offering and other required reports/issuances in the Full
Disclosure Policy Board as well as in conspicuous places within the Municipality
for proper transparency. We further recommended that the concerned offices fast
track the preparation of reports for submission, consolidation and prompt posting.

We recommended and management agreed on the following:

a. Ensure that only the PPAs culled from the Local Development Investment
Plan are included in the Annual Investment Plan and Annual LDRRMF Plan
to ensure that the implemented PPAs contributed to the achievement of long-
term goals.
b. Require the persons responsible in the preparation of the planning documents
to completely supply the details required in the template to facilitate its
accomplishments.
Henceforth, the Municipal Council should prepare a well-defined AIP in
accordance with the harmonization of local planning, investment
programming, revenue administration, budgeting, and expenditure
management guidelines prescribed in the DBM-NEDA-DILG-DOF Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 1, series of 2007 dated March 8, 2007.

We recommended and management agreed on the following:

1. Require the MDRRMO to closely monitor the undertaking of proposed
projects funded out of LDRRMF to avoid/minimize disaster projected and
unexpected risks, and enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities
at all levels.
2. Provide for the specific dates such as the start and end dates for each PPA and
ensure that all PPAs are provided with the appropriate implementation
schedules.
3. A comprehensive MDRRMF Plan should be prepared to include both the
current appropriations and unexpended balance in the Special Trust Fund.
4. Prepare an adjusting entry to correct the entry made in reverting the CY 2013
unexpended balance as follows:
GF Book TF Book
DR Prior Period Adjustment Cash in Bank - LCCA
CR Cash in Bank - LCCA Trust Liability – DRRMF

The balances of the SET Receivable and Deferred SET were the
remaining amounts when the NGAS was replaced by PPSAS. The amounts
were originally set up by the COA personnel from Central and Regional Offices
when eNGAS was installed in 2007. The installation which created the said
accounts in the General Fund had made the management believed that the same
is proper and valid. However, the management assured the team that necessary
measures to rectify the situation will be undertaken.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Confirm the amount of the payables from the list of the identified creditors
to determine which of the payables are still valid and bonafide obligation;
b. Drop from the books of accounts the undocumented obligations /
payables;
c. Make necessary adjustments in the books of accounts if proven to be
without existing creditors for an accurate presentation of the payable
account in the Financial Statements.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive
direct the Municipal Treasurer to do the following to protect the very liquid asset
of the Municipality:
a) Deposit all her collections intact with the Municipality’s authorized
depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day;
b) Deposit partially collections made during the last working day of the year
to the depository bank.
c) Double check deposits made to minimize adjustments

The management was amenable on the findings and promised that the
assistance of job orders to act as collectors in behalf of regular employees will
instantly be stopped and liquidation of collections will strictly be demanded by
the end of the day or the once the amount reaches P5,000.00. Also, since the
Municipality really lacks personnel that could be bonded in the Treasury Office, it
was provisioned to include in the office budget proposal for CY 2019 the request
for additional collectors/or treasury personnel because the existing collectors are
oftentimes utilized in the distribution of tax bills and examination of books of
accounts of businessmen thus reduces the number of collectors in the Office and
would cause congestion of taxpayers in the lobby of the Municipality.

Moreover, the Municipal Treasurer commented that the system used by the
Municipality allows only a one-time preparation of the Report on Collections
and Deposits (RCD) thus during the day when cut offs were set per collector to
liquidate collections, the system will automatically change the date to the
following day. In such cases, upon request of the collector, the system
administrator will edit the date for this reason some of the dates in the system
were erroneously recorded by one (1) day. However, measures will be undertaken
to correct the system and communication will be made with the developer to
make the changes.

3. Strictly observe the guidelines set forth by the NDRRM-DILG-DBM-CSC
Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-01 dated April 4, 2014.

Recommendations:
1. The management should instruct the Municipal Planning and Development
Coordinator and the Municipal Development Council to closely monitor the
utilization and implementation of the projects embodied in the Annual
Investment Plan.
2. Likewise, instruct the implementing department to fast track the implementation
based on the scheduled time frames to achieve the desired outcome of the
management.
3. Strict compliance to Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 of the Department
of Interior and Local Government and Department of Budget and Management
is advised.

1. Require the Municipal Accountant to investigate and record the reconciling items in the subsidiary ledger to reflect the adjusted balances of the Cash-In-Bank account.
2. Corresponding JEV should be drawn and copy furnished the Auditor’s Office to effect the recording adjustments.
3. Strictly adhere to the pertinent provisions of NGAS for LGU Volume 1 in the reversal of stale checks.
observations.reason: Adjustments for reconciling items were still on going. Vouching of necessary documents needed for adjustments were still on process.

observations.recommendation:
1. Strictly observe the applicable guidelines set forth by the NGAS Manual for LGU and other related rules and regulations.
2. Provide adequate storage facilities to store and safe keep all procured supplies and materials so as to protect them from possible loss or wastage.
observations.reason: Construction of storage facilities for supplies and materials were still on process.

observations.recommendation:
1. Require the Municipal School Board to strictly comply with Section 100 and 272 of the Local Government Code of 1991 in identifying programs/projects/activities chargeable against SEF.
2. Refrain from charging expenditures not related to the purpose of the SEF to avoid future disallowances.

Require the BAC to prepare the Procurement Monitoring Report for submission to the HOPE for approval and copy furnished the GPPB and the Auditor’s Office. With this, we could verify the status of procurement process and activities as basis for our audit.

1. Create a Disposal Committee to be headed by the GSO to record all the unserviceable and obsolete properties to the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties, as basis for transfer, appraisal, and disposal.
2. Dispose the unserviceable properties immediately pursuant to Section 79 of PD 1445 to prevent further deterioration and incurring other carrying costs in storing these items.
Require the Municipal Treasurer to return or remit immediately the remaining PDAF of the Municipality amounting P6,119,574.76 to Bureau of Treasury.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials be instructed sternly to submit the required financial reports and accounts. Otherwise, the sanctions, as provided in Section 122 of P.D. 1445, shall be imposed.
observations.reason: There were still delays in the submission of vouchers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant be instructed to submit without delay all overdue financial reports and its supporting schedules, pursuant to the provision of Section 70 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs) so as to enable the Auditor to fully conduct an audit of accounts of the Municipality. Also, require the Municipal Budget Officer to submit Status of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (SAAO) to the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer adhere strictly to Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB) Resolution No. 05-2010 dated October 29, 2010 and Department of Interior and Local Government (DILG) Memorandum Circular No. 2012-032 dated February 15, 2012 on the printing of the Accountable Forms to be undertaken by government printers, so as to avoid any complications to the future.

We recommend that management require the Municipal Accountant to update the preparation of Bank Reconciliation Statements for all depository accounts. Locate and investigate the discrepancies noted and effect the corrections by making necessary adjustments on affected accounts.
observations.reason: Submission of BRS and its Bank Statements were still delayed.

observations.recommendation: The LGU should adhere strictly with the rules and regulations prescribed in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, pursuant to Section 89 of PD 1445, COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997. The LGU should strictly implement the said rule to expand its monitoring system and internal control over cash.
observations.reason: One of the accountable officers Mr. Limpin, former MLGOO of the Municipality and presently the DILG Provincial Director of Sarangani Province was not able to submit liquidation for his travel. Cash advances granted are liquidated as soon as the purpose is served.
